templates.name: Start Blank
templates.tagline: Start from a blank canvas and define your own operating system from scratch.

templates.name: The Long-Term Operator
templates.tagline: Run today like Day 1, decide for a 20-year horizon, and protect your peak hours like the scarce resource they are.

seed.tagline: Decide on a 20-year horizon. Protect the morning. Stay close to the customer.

mind.title: Regret minimization
mind.what: When a decision feels heavy, ask yourself which choice you will regret less from 80.
mind.why: Loss aversion is loudest in the present and quiet in retrospect. Future-you is a calmer judge of what matters.

routine.title: Protect the peak block
routine.what: Block your best 2–4 hours each day for decisions and writing. No meetings allowed in the block.
routine.why: Decision quality is energy-bound. You only get one peak block per day; the rest of the day flows downhill from it.

vision.title: Day 1 mentality
vision.what: Treat every day as the company's first day. Stay hungry, stay close to the customer, fight stasis.
vision.why: Big organisations decay by drifting from customers into process. Day 1 is the permanent posture that prevents it.

communication.title: Write in full prose
communication.what: Major proposals as 4–6 page memos, not slides. Read them silently together at the start of the meeting.
communication.why: Bullets hide weak thinking. Prose exposes it. Silent reading means everyone enters discussion with shared context.

templates.name: The First-Principles Builder
templates.tagline: Reduce every problem to physics, attack with the smallest possible technical team, and refuse analogy as a shortcut.

seed.tagline: Reason from physics, not analogy. Small teams. Question every requirement.

mind.title: First-principles reasoning
mind.what: Decompose every problem to its physical or mathematical constraints. Reason up; refuse reasoning by analogy.
mind.why: Analogies inherit assumptions. The biggest cost reductions live in noticing an assumption everyone else accepts as given.

mind.title: 5-step engineering process
mind.what: Question every requirement → delete it → simplify what remains → accelerate cycle time → automate. In that order.
mind.why: Most engineers skip to automation. The first four steps are where the wins live. Doing them in order keeps you from optimising things that shouldn't exist.

team.title: Small expert teams
team.what: Thin layers of senior individual contributors. Skip-level access is the default, not an exception.
team.why: Big engineering orgs lose resolution at every layer. The smaller the team, the less translation, the better the decisions.

vision.title: Mission > company
vision.what: Frame the company around a civilisational mission, not a market opportunity.
vision.why: Talented engineers will burn out chasing a quarterly target. They won't burn out chasing a goal they believe matters.

templates.name: The Founder-Mode CEO
templates.tagline: Stay in the details. Refuse to delegate the thing the company is. Run product reviews at every layer.

seed.tagline: In the details. Design-led. Skip levels. Taste is the company.

mind.title: Stay in the details
mind.what: Reject the playbook of 'hire great people and get out of their way'. Stay deeply involved in product, design, and operating decisions.
mind.why: Taste is what holds a company together past 1,000 people, and taste cannot be delegated. Founder mode is the discipline of staying the company's eye on the work.

mind.title: Say no to a thousand things
mind.what: Treat focus as subtractive. Kill more than you launch.
mind.why: Focus isn't choosing what to do — it's choosing what to refuse. Every yes costs the time you could have spent doing the better thing instead.

team.title: Functional org, not divisional
team.what: Organise senior leadership by function (design, engineering, product) with central ownership of the roadmap.
team.why: Divisional structures optimise locally at the cost of company-level coherence. Functional structures force the leadership team to argue priorities together.

team.title: A-players only
team.what: Hire only A-players. A-players hire other A-players; B-players hire C-players to feel superior.
team.why: Talent density compounds or decays — there's no flat. Once you let in B-players, the org spirals downward.

communication.title: Skip-level reviews
communication.what: Run product and design reviews directly, regardless of layers. Weigh in on copy, visuals, animations.
communication.why: Detail is fractal. The product's quality equals the median quality of every small decision underneath. Caring publicly about details calibrates the bar without a policy memo.

culture.title: Design as integrative discipline
culture.what: Treat design as the brief, not the polish layer applied at the end.
culture.why: When design is a department, it competes with engineering. When it's the brief, it's what everyone is building toward.

templates.name: The Empathetic Reformer
templates.tagline: Lead with listening. Treat empathy as a source of innovation. Make growth mindset the cultural posture.

seed.tagline: Learn-it-all over know-it-all. Empathy as a strategy. Listen first.

mind.title: Growth mindset
mind.what: Adopt 'learn-it-all' over 'know-it-all' as the cultural posture.
mind.why: Stalled organisations are usually know-it-all problems: smart people defending past correctness. Growth mindset gives the company permission to be curious again.

mind.title: Empathy as innovation
mind.what: Treat empathy as the ability to perceive unmet, unarticulated needs — not as a soft skill.
mind.why: Without empathy you can only ship better versions of what already exists. The breakthroughs come from sensing what people need but cannot yet say.

body.title: Daily quiet time
body.what: Build a daily ritual — meditation, journaling, or just silence — that gives the mind a place to land.
body.why: Senior leadership has enormous social load. Without a daily reset, the next ten hours of intense people-work breaks you.

team.title: Personally onboard every new hire
team.what: Tell the founding story and the vision yourself, every time someone joins.
team.why: Culture is set by what the founder repeats. Outsourcing the welcome outsources the canonical story — and the company drifts.

vision.title: Performance with purpose
vision.what: Reject the framing that profit and purpose are in tension. Treat them as the same long-horizon equation.
vision.why: Long-term shareholder value requires healthy products, sustainable supply chains, and a workforce that wants to be there. They are not separate strategies.

communication.title: Listening tour as day one
communication.what: Spend your first weeks in a new role listening — to customers, partners, employees — rather than announcing.
communication.why: Listening first earns trust to make the harder calls later. The first 90 days is the only time you can ask everything.

templates.name: The Network Catalyst
templates.tagline: Build the network before you need it. Ship before you're ready. Bet on speed in winner-take-all markets.

seed.tagline: Permanent beta. Network first. Speed over polish.

mind.title: Permanent beta
mind.what: Treat yourself, your career, and your company as permanently in beta — always shipping, always learning, never declaring done.
mind.why: Industries change faster than careers used to. Permanent beta is the posture that makes you legible to that change.

mind.title: The contrarian question
mind.what: Hold one filtering question: 'What important truth do very few people agree with me on?'
mind.why: The answers are also the seeds of the only businesses worth building. The question filters for conviction and original thinking.

team.title: Tour-of-duty alliance
team.what: Frame employment as 2–4 year tours of duty with explicit missions and mutual investment.
team.why: The lifelong-loyalty contract died decades ago. Naming the alliance honestly produces a better deal on both sides.

vision.title: Blitzscale where it matters
vision.what: In winner-take-all markets, prioritise speed over efficiency. Accept high burn and operational chaos as the price.
vision.why: Being second in a network-effect market is being nothing. Speed to first scale is the only durable competitive advantage in that shape of game.

vision.title: Last-mover advantage
vision.what: Aim to be the last entrant in a category — the one whose position can't be displaced — rather than the first.
vision.why: First movers are usually displaced. The durable position is owning the standard, brand, or network. Plan for the endgame, not the launch.

communication.title: Network before you need it
communication.what: Invest in relationships in slack time — when nothing is at stake — rather than when you need something.
communication.why: Transactional networks don't work. By the time you 'need' your network, it's too late to build it.
